en.extract:
The lemon dove or cinnamon dove (Aplopelia larvata) is a species of bird in the pigeon family Columbidae found in montane forests of sub-Saharan Africa. The lemon dove has a generally brownish-grey plumage with a cinnamon brown breast. Adults have a greenish-glossed neck and white markings on the head, and juveniles are rather more brown with pale feather fringe barring and have greyer facial markings. This dove is a common species, and the International Union for Conservation of Nature has rated its conservation status as being of "least concern".


Taxonomy
The lemon dove was previously placed in the genus Columba, but it differs from the African pigeons of the genus Columba both genetically, in which it is closer to the genus Streptopelia, and by its more strongly terrestrial habits. The population on the island of São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea, usually considered a subspecies, has been treated as a separate species A. simplex (São Tomé lemon dove; syn. C. simplex) by some authors.
Seven subspecies are recognised:

A. l. inornata Reichenow, A, 1827 – Sierra Leone to Liberia, southeastern Nigeria, Cameroon, and Gabon; Bioko
A. l. principalis (Hartlaub, KJG, 1866) – Príncipe (Gulf of Guinea)
A. l. simplex (Hartlaub, KJG, 1849) – São Tomé (Gulf of Guinea)
A. l. bronzina (Rüppell, WPES, 1837) – Eritrea, Ethiopia, and southeastern South Sudan (Boma Hills)
A. l. larvata (Temminck, CJ, 1809) – southern Sudan and central and eastern Uganda to South Africa
A. l. jacksoni Sharpe, RB, 1904 – eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo and southwestern Uganda to western Tanzania
A. l. samaliyae White, CMN, 1948 – Angola, southern Democratic Republic of the Congo, and northwestern Zambia


Description

The lemon dove is fairly small, measuring 24–30 cm (9.4–11.8 in) in length and weighing 81.7–150 g (2.88–5.29 oz). Adults have a plumage that is dark brown above, weakly glossed iridescent greenish to bronze (depending on light angle) on the sides of the neck, and cinnamon brown below, and with a pale whitish face grading into the brown body plumage. The feet, iris and orbital skin are red, the bill is black. Males and females are very similar, though females slightly duller. Juveniles are less iridescent, have lighter brown plumage with pale fringes on the wing covert and body feathers giving a barred appearance, and duller grey facial markings. The western African subspecies have greyer plumage than those of southern and eastern Africa.


Distribution
The lemon dove has a very wide range and is distributed in montane forests in sub-Saharan Africa, ranging from 100 to 3,000 metres (330 to 9,840 ft) altitude in eastern Africa. The diet consists mainly of various small fruits, seeds, molluscs and insects. The female usually lays two creamy white eggs.


Status and behaviour

Widespread throughout its range, the population trend of the lemon dove is stable and the bird is evaluated as a species of least concern on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species. In southern Africa, it is fairly common in evergreen forests and plantations close to the south and east coasts, where it mainly feeds on the ground; it is shy and unobtrusive, often heard but not easily seen. It seems to be declining in certain parts of its range; in Tanzania it is not rare above 1,300 metres (4,300 ft) altitude in the Nguu North Forest Reserve, but not at all common in some lower-lying habitat. It is nearly absent from the southeast of that country.


References


Sources
Jensen, Flemming P.; Tøttrup, Anders P.; Christensen, Kim D. (2005). "The avifauna of coastal forests in southeast Tanzania" (PDF). Scopus. 25: 1–22. Archived from the original (PDF) on 2 October 2006.
Seddon, N.; Ekstrom, J.M.M.; Capper, D.R.; Isherwood, I.S.; Muna, R.; Pople, R.G.; Tarimo, E.; Timothy, J. (1999). "The importance of the Nilo and Nguu North Forest Reserves for the conservation of montane forest birds in Tanzania" (PDF). Biological Conservation. 87 (1): 59–72. Bibcode:1999BCons..87...59S. doi:10.1016/S0006-3207(98)00041-X. Archived from the original (PDF) on 14 February 2012.
Sinclair, Ian; Hockey, Phil; Tarboton, Warwick R. (2002). SASOL Birds of Southern Africa. Cape Town: Struik. ISBN 1-86872-721-1.


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet
(Cinnamon dove = ) lemon dove - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds

The '''lemon dove''' or '''cinnamon dove''' ('''''Aplopelia larvata''''') is a [[species]] of bird in the pigeon [[family (biology)|family]] [[Columbidae]] found in montane forests of sub-Saharan Africa. The lemon dove has a generally brownish-grey plumage with a cinnamon brown breast. Adults have a greenish-glossed neck and white markings on the head, and juveniles are rather more brown with pale feather fringe barring and have greyer facial markings. This dove is a common species, and the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] has rated its conservation status as being of "[[Least-concern species|least concern]]".

==Taxonomy==
The lemon dove was previously placed in the genus ''[[Columba (genus)|Columba]]'',<ref>{{cite web | title=Pigeons – IOC World Bird List | website=IOC World Bird List – Version 15.1 | date=2025-02-20 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/pigeons/ | access-date=2026-01-07}}</ref> but it differs from the African pigeons of the genus ''Columba'' both genetically, in which it is closer to the genus ''[[Streptopelia]]'', and by its more strongly terrestrial habits.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Boyd | first1=B.M. | last2=Nguyen | first2=N.-P. | last3=Allen | first3=J.M. | last4=Waterhouse | first4=R.M. | last5=Vo | first5=K.B. | last6=Sweet | first6=A.D. | last7=Clayton | first7=D.H. | last8=Bush | first8=S.E. | last9=Shapiro | first9=M.D. | last10=Johnson | first10=K.P. | date=2022 | title=Long-distance dispersal of pigeons and doves generated new ecological opportunities for host-switching and adaptive radiation by their parasites | journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences | volume=289 | issue=20220042 | pages=1-8 | doi=10.1098/rspb.2022.0042 | doi-access=free | pmc=8905168 }}</ref><ref name=avilist/> The population on the island of [[São Tomé (island)|São Tomé]] in the Gulf of Guinea, usually considered a subspecies,<ref name=avilist/> has been treated as a separate species ''A. simplex'' (São Tomé lemon dove; syn. ''C. simplex'') by some authors.<ref name="Hoyo">{{cite book | last1=Hoyo | first1=Josep del | last2=Elliott | first2=Andrew | last3=Sargatal | first3=Jordi | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | publisher=Lynx Edicions | publication-place=Barcelona | date=1997 | isbn=84-87334-22-9 | volume=4 | pages=60–61, 131–132}}</ref>

Seven [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=7 November 2025 }}</ref>
* ''A. l. inornata'' [[Anton Reichenow|Reichenow, A]], 1827 – Sierra Leone to Liberia, southeastern Nigeria, Cameroon, and Gabon; [[Bioko]]
* ''A. l. principalis'' ([[Gustav Hartlaub|Hartlaub, KJG]], 1866) – [[Príncipe]] ([[Gulf of Guinea]])
* ''A. l. simplex'' (Hartlaub, KJG, 1849) – [[São Tomé (island)|São Tomé]] (Gulf of Guinea)
* ''A. l. bronzina'' ([[Eduard Rüppell|Rüppell, WPES]], 1837) – [[Eritrea]], Ethiopia, and southeastern South Sudan (Boma Hills)
* ''A. l. larvata'' ([[Coenraad Jacob Temminck|Temminck, CJ]], 1809) – southern Sudan and central and eastern Uganda to South Africa
* ''A. l. jacksoni'' [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe, RB]], 1904 – eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo and southwestern Uganda to western Tanzania
* ''A. l. samaliyae'' White, CMN, 1948 – Angola, southern Democratic Republic of the Congo, and northwestern Zambia

==Description==
[[File:Aplopelia larvata, Cape Town, South Africa 05.jpg|left|thumb|''A. l. larvata'' at Cape Town, South Africa]]
The lemon dove is fairly small, measuring {{cvt|24|-|30|cm|in}} in length and weighing {{cvt|81.7|-|150|g|oz}}.<ref name = "CRC">''CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses'' by John B. Dunning Jr. (Editor). CRC Press (1992), {{ISBN|978-0-8493-4258-5}}.</ref> Adults have a plumage that is dark brown above, weakly glossed [[iridescence|iridescent]] greenish to bronze (depending on light angle) on the sides of the neck, and cinnamon brown below, and with a pale whitish face grading into the brown body plumage. The feet, [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]] and orbital skin are red, the [[Beak|bill]] is black. Males and females are very similar, though females slightly duller. Juveniles are less iridescent, have lighter brown plumage with pale fringes on the wing covert and body feathers giving a barred appearance, and duller grey facial markings. The western African [[subspecies]] have greyer plumage than those of southern and eastern Africa.<ref name="Zimmerman">{{cite book | last1=Zimmerman | first1=Dale Allen | last2=Pearson | first2=David J. | last3=Turner | first3=Donald A. | title=Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania | publisher=Christopher Helm Publishers, Incorporated | date=2002 | isbn=0-7136-6305-7 | pages=116, 361}}</ref><ref name="Newman">{{cite book | last=Newman | first=Kenneth | title=Newman's Birds of Southern Africa | publisher=Southern Book Pub of South Africa | publication-place=Halfway House | date=1998-12-01 | isbn=1-86812-757-5 | page=198}}</ref>{{sfn|Sinclair|Hockey|Tarboton|2002}}

==Distribution==
The lemon dove has a very wide range and is distributed in [[montane forest]]s in sub-Saharan [[Africa]], ranging from {{convert|100|to|3,000|m|ft|-1}} [[Above mean sea level|altitude]] in eastern Africa. The diet consists mainly of various small fruits, seeds, molluscs and insects. The female usually lays two creamy white eggs.{{sfn|Sinclair|Hockey|Tarboton|2002}}{{sfn|Jensen|Tøttrup|Christensen|2005}}

==Status and behaviour==
[[File:Aplopelia larvata simplex, São Tomé 01.jpg|left|thumb|''A. l. simplex'' on São Tomé]]
Widespread throughout its range, the population trend of the lemon dove is stable and the bird is evaluated as a species of [[least concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]] of Threatened Species.<ref name=IUCN/> In southern Africa, it is fairly common in evergreen forests and plantations close to the south and east coasts, where it mainly feeds on the ground; it is shy and unobtrusive, often heard but not easily seen.<ref name="Newman"/> It seems to be declining in certain parts of its range; in [[Tanzania]] it is not rare above {{convert|1300|m}} altitude in the [[Nguu North Forest Reserve]], but not at all common in some lower-lying [[Habitat (ecology)|habitat]]. It is nearly absent from the southeast of that country.{{sfn|Seddon|Ekstrom|Capper|Isherwood|1999}}{{sfn|Jensen|Tøttrup|Christensen|2005}}<ref name=IUCN/>

zh.extract:
非洲鸽（学名：Columba larvata），是鸽形目鸠鸽科鸽属的鸟类。


特征
非洲鸽体长约24至30厘米，体重81.7至150克。雄鸟上体为暗棕色，颈侧具绿色金属光泽，胸部呈肉桂色，头部带有明显的白色斑纹。脚、虹膜及眼周裸露皮肤为红色，喙为黑色，整体对比清晰。雌鸟及未成年的雄鸟外形相近，但羽色较浅，多为淡棕色，面部白色标记转为暗灰色。
